survivors.title: Local-URI guard rejects Unix-domain-socket Postgres host (most data-sovereign connection is unusable)
survivors.claim: require_local_uri() rejects a libpq host that is a Unix-domain-socket directory. Verified empirically: require_local_uri('host=/var/run/postgresql', what='x'), require_local_uri('host=/tmp', what='x'), and require_local_uri('postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql', what='x') all raise NonLocalEndpointError. The cause: when a host/hostaddr value is found, the code calls is_loopback_host(h), which returns False for any string that is neither 'localhost' nor a parseable loopback IP (is_loopback_host('/var/run/postgresql') == False). A leading-'/' host is libpq's standard syntax for connecting over a local Unix-domain socket directory — a connection that never touches the network and is strictly more data-sovereign than TCP loopback, yet it is the one local form the guard refuses. The default _DEFAULT_PG in checkpoint.py uses TCP loopback so the default works, but any operator who sets ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL to the conventional local-socket form gets a hard failure from get_postgres_connection_string().
survivors.why_real: This is a falsifiable availability/correctness defect, not a style nit: an operator following standard local-Postgres practice (peer-auth over /var/run/postgresql) cannot start the checkpointer at all — it raises NonLocalEndpointError at point of use. The guard's stated purpose (ADR-0003: keep data on-box) is fully satisfied by a Unix socket; rejecting it both blocks a legitimate config and is logically inconsistent with the guard's own goal. There is no test or doc declaring this rejection intentional (grep of tests/ and docs/ found none).
survivors.suggested_fix: In require_local_uri, before the is_loopback_host check on a host/hostaddr value, treat an absolute filesystem path (value startswith('/'), and not a UNC '//'/'\\' double-separator prefix) as a local Unix-domain socket directory and accept it. Apply the same allowance in both the libpq keyword/value DSN branch (line ~146) and the query-parameter branch (line ~134-141). Keep the existing UNC double-separator rejection so '//server/share' still fails closed. Add a regression test asserting host=/var/run/postgresql is accepted while host=//evil/share is rejected.

refute.reasoning:
Attempted to refute by reading src/assay_engine/_local.py and the call site, and by empirical reproduction. Could not refute; the finding holds on every point.

EMPIRICAL: Confirmed exactly. is_loopback_host('/var/run/postgresql') == False, and require_local_uri raises NonLocalEndpointError for 'host=/var/run/postgresql', 'host=/tmp', and 'postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql'.

ROOT CAUSE: Confirmed. is_loopback_host (_local.py:23-38) accepts only literal 'localhost' or a string ipaddress.ip_address() can parse as loopback. A leading-'/' Unix-domain-socket directory parses as neither, returns False. Both the DSN branch (lines 144-153) and the authority/query branch (134-141) feed host/hostaddr values through is_loopback_host, so the local-socket form is rejected in all forms.

REACHABILITY: Confirmed real production path. checkpoint.py:162-163 routes operator-supplied ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL through require_local_uri inside get_postgres_connection_string(), called at checkpoint.py:246 when building the checkpointer. An operator setting the conventional peer-auth socket DSN gets a hard NonLocalEndpointError at point of use.

NOT OUT-OF-SCOPE: This is not optional/import-guarded, not a scrubbed transcript, not dataset-agnostic. It is core persistence config. The default _DEFAULT_PG uses TCP loopback so the default works, masking the bug for default deployments, but the documented/standard local-socket form fails.

INTENTIONALITY: grep of tests/ and docs/ confirms no test or doc asserts socket rejection is intended. tests/test_persistence.py exercises TCP loopback, remote-host rejection, multi-host, query-override, UNC paths, and whitespace DSNs, but never a Unix-socket host — consistent with the finding's claim that this case was simply not considered.

LOGICAL CONSISTENCY: The guard's stated purpose (ADR-0003: keep data on-box) is strictly better satisfied by a Unix domain socket (never touches the network) than by TCP loopback (which is accepted). Rejecting the socket form while accepting loopback is self-inconsistent and blocks a legitimate, more-sovereign config. This is a falsifiable availability/correctness defect, not a style nit.

survivors.title: SIGTERM handler swallows process termination when no prior Python handler exists
survivors.claim: When tracing is bootstrapped on the main thread and no prior Python SIGTERM handler is installed, signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM) returns signal.SIG_DFL, which is NOT callable. The installed _handler flushes/shuts down the span provider and then evaluates `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)` — which is False for SIG_DFL — so it returns WITHOUT terminating. Because it replaced the default SIGTERM disposition (terminate the process), the process no longer dies on SIGTERM. Verified: `python -c "import signal; p=signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM); print(callable(p), p is signal.SIG_DFL)"` prints `False True`. bootstrap_tracing() runs inside run_study() and orchestration.run()/resume(), so any normally-started traced run on the main thread installs this handler.
survivors.why_real: SIGTERM is the standard graceful-stop signal: `docker stop`, `kubectl delete pod`, systemd `stop`, and plain `kill` all send it. After this handler is installed, the process ignores SIGTERM's terminate action and keeps running until the orchestrator escalates to SIGKILL after its grace period (typically 10-30s). Every run hangs on shutdown, and an operator's `kill <pid>` silently fails. The unit test (tests/test_observability.py:211-229) even monkeypatches getsignal to SIG_DFL and invokes the handler, but only asserts flush/shutdown were called — it never asserts the process would still terminate, so the defect is uncaught. This directly contradicts the docstring intent ("a main-thread SIGTERM handler also flushes+shuts down"): it flushes but then prevents termination.
survivors.suggested_fix: After flush+shutdown, re-raise the default disposition for SIG_DFL/SIG_IGN instead of only chaining a callable prior handler. E.g.: if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame); elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL: signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_DFL); os.kill(os.getpid(), signal.SIGTERM)  # re-deliver to take the default (terminate) action. (SIG_IGN can simply return.) Add a test asserting that, with prior=SIG_DFL, the handler re-establishes/forwards termination rather than swallowing it.

refute.reasoning:
I attempted to refute finding K-REL-1 but it holds on every verifiable point.

CODE (src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:136-158): _install_flush_on_exit captures `prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)` and installs `_handler`, which does `_safe(flush)`, `_safe(provider.shutdown)`, then `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)`. There is NO else branch — no os._exit, no signal.raise_signal, no re-raise, no restoration of default disposition. When no prior Python handler exists, prior is signal.SIG_DFL.

VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY: `python3 -c "import signal; p=signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM); ..."` prints `callable: False is SIG_DFL: True`. So in the common no-prior-handler case, the `if callable(prior)` branch is skipped and the handler returns. The default SIGTERM disposition (terminate the process) has been replaced by a handler that flushes and then keeps running. SIGTERM is effectively swallowed.

REACHABILITY: bootstrap_tracing() is invoked in real run/resume paths — pipeline.py:381, orchestration/graph.py:111 and :139. Tracing is ENABLED BY DEFAULT: _disabled() (tracing.py:53-54) returns True only when ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING is explicitly set. The handler is installed whenever bootstrap runs on the main thread (threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread()), which is the normal CLI/orchestrator entrypoint case.

TEST GAP CONFIRMED: tests/test_observability.py:211-229 monkeypatches getsignal to SIG_DFL, installs the handler, invokes it, and asserts only calls["shutdown"] >= 1 (and flush). It never asserts the process would still terminate, so the regression is uncaught — exactly as the finding states.

NO MITIGATION FOUND: grepped/read the handler region; nothing restores termination in the SIG_DFL path. This is not a false positive, not already-mitigated, not out-of-scope-by-design (it is core process-lifecycle behavior, not dataset/backend-specific), and is a genuine production reliability defect: docker stop / kubectl delete pod / systemd stop / plain kill send SIGTERM, which will now be swallowed until SIGKILL escalation. Real, high-severity, CONC dimension. The only nuance softening "every run hangs" is that an operator could set ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING or another library could have pre-installed a SIGTERM handler; neither is the default, so the defect manifests in normal operation. Finding STANDS.

survivors.title: Lockfile-sync "reproducibility guard" re-resolves against live PyPI (no --exclude-newer); already failing on a clean checkout
survivors.location: .github/workflows/ci.yml:126-146 (audit job, "Lockfiles are in sync" step) + scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh:31-33
survivors.claim: The gate runs `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes` and diffs the result against the committed requirements.lock. Because most pins are transitive and pyproject uses ranges (e.g. mlflow>=3.1,<4, arize-phoenix>=17,<18), and there is no `--exclude-newer <timestamp>` / resolution-date pin anywhere, `uv pip compile` resolves each transitive dep to the NEWEST version satisfying the range at the moment CI runs. The committed lock is therefore compared against a freshly re-resolved lock that picks up any upstream release published since the lock was committed. I reproduced this: on a clean checkout today the gate FAILS — committed requirements.lock pins arize-phoenix==17.6.0, certifi==2026.5.20, mlflow==3.13.0, mlflow-skinny/tracing==3.13.0, tqdm==4.68.2, whereas a fresh `uv pip compile` resolves arize-phoenix==17.7.0, certifi==2026.6.17, mlflow==3.14.0, tqdm==4.68.3. requirements.lock is reported STALE.
survivors.why_real: This makes the merge gate (all-checks fans in `audit`) go red on EVERY pull request the instant any of ~218 transitive packages publishes an in-range release — regardless of whether the PR touches dependencies at all. It both spuriously blocks all merges and defeats the stated purpose: a guard meant to prove reproducibility is itself irreproducible (its expected value changes with wall-clock time and live PyPI state). It is currently failing, so main cannot be merged into until someone regenerates the lock, and it will re-break on the next upstream release.
survivors.suggested_fix: Pin the resolution timestamp so the regen is deterministic: add `--exclude-newer <ISO-date>` to every `uv pip compile` invocation in both ci.yml and regenerate_lockfiles.sh, recording the date in a file (or read it from the lock header). Then the committed lock only changes when pyproject ranges or the exclude-newer date change, and the gate compares like-for-like. Alternatively, drop the regen-and-diff gate entirely and instead verify the committed lock still satisfies pyproject (`uv pip compile --no-upgrade` / `uv lock --check`) rather than re-resolving to newest.

refute.reasoning:
I attempted to refute K-OPS-1 by reading the code and reproducing the gate; it holds up on every point.

VERIFICATION:
1. The gate (.github/workflows/ci.yml:126-146) runs `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes` and diffs (grep -v '^#') the result against the committed requirements.lock, failing via `|| { echo ::error...; exit 1; }`. There is NO `--exclude-newer`, no `UV_EXCLUDE_NEWER`, no resolution-date pin anywhere in ci.yml, regenerate_lockfiles.sh, or the lock header — grep confirms only the uv *binary version* (0.11.21) is pinned, which controls output FORMAT but not which package versions get resolved.
2. pyproject.toml uses ranges for direct deps (arize-phoenix>=17,<18, mlflow>=3.1,<4) and dependencies=[] with optional extras; transitive deps are unconstrained. So `uv pip compile` resolves to the newest in-range release at wall-clock time.
3. EMPIRICAL REPRODUCTION (with CI-pinned uv 0.11.21 on a clean checkout): committed requirements.lock pins arize-phoenix==17.6.0, certifi==2026.5.20, mlflow/mlflow-skinny/mlflow-tracing==3.13.0, tqdm==4.68.2. A fresh `uv pip compile` re-resolves to arize-phoenix==17.7.0, certifi==2026.6.17, mlflow*==3.14.0, tqdm==4.68.3 — EXACTLY the drift the finding named. The diff returns exit 1 (differ), so the gate step fails. These are the only changed pins (48 diff lines, 6 version bumps).
4. The audit job has no continue-on-error and is a hard `needs:` of `all-checks` (ci.yml:216-231), which is the single required branch-protection status check and fails if any need is non-success.

CONSEQUENCE: a guard intended to prove reproducibility is itself irreproducible (expected value changes with live PyPI state), and it is currently RED on a clean checkout, blocking ALL merges into main until someone regenerates the lock — and it will re-break on the next in-range upstream release of any of ~218 transitive packages, regardless of whether a PR touches dependencies. This is a genuine OPS defect, not a false positive, not mitigated, and not out-of-scope by design. The finding STANDS.

Note: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh has the identical un-dated resolution behavior, so even the documented remediation only resets the clock; it does not make the gate deterministic. A real fix would add `--exclude-newer <committed-timestamp>` (or a uv.lock with a recorded resolution time) to pin the resolution date.

Relevant files: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml (lines 126-146 gate, 216-231 all-checks fan-in), /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh (lines 21-22), /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml (ranged deps), /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/requirements.lock.

survivors.title: Documented lockfile-regeneration script omits requirements-build.lock, which CI gates — remediation cannot fix a build-lock staleness
survivors.claim: The CI audit job (ci.yml:142-146) diffs requirements-build.lock against a fresh `uv pip compile <(printf 'hatchling\neditables\n') --universal --generate-hashes` and fails with `::error::requirements-build.lock is stale`. The single documented remediation — referenced by both .github/dependabot.yml (#G-016 note) and the CI error messages — is scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh, but `grep -c requirements-build.lock scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` returns 0: the script only emits requirements.lock and requirements-core.lock. There is no committed script path that regenerates the build lock.
survivors.why_real: requirements-build.lock pins hatchling and its transitive deps including trove-classifiers==2026.6.1.19, which has a frequent (effectively per-release) cadence. When hatchling/trove-classifiers/packaging/pluggy publishes a new version, the build-lock sync gate fails, and a maintainer following the documented `scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` workflow will regenerate the other two locks, re-run CI, and still be red with no scripted way to fix it — they must hand-reconstruct the exact CI command from the ci.yml comment. This is a real, reachable maintenance dead-end (the build lock is only in sync today by luck of timing).
survivors.suggested_fix: Add the build-lock regeneration to scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh, byte-for-byte matching the CI command: `printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in && uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-build.lock`, so the one documented remediation covers all three gated lockfiles.

refute.reasoning:
All factual claims verified against the actual code:

1. scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh (lines 21-24) regenerates ONLY requirements.lock (--all-extras) and requirements-core.lock (--extra dev). `grep -c requirements-build.lock` returns 0 — the build lock is never emitted.

2. .github/workflows/ci.yml gates requirements-build.lock: it runs `uv pip compile <(printf 'hatchling\neditables\n') --universal --generate-hashes`, diffs against the committed lock, and fails hard with `::error::requirements-build.lock is stale ...` + `exit 1`.

3. .github/dependabot.yml line 9 (#G-016 note) instructs maintainers to run `scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` and commit the result to make a Dependabot PR mergeable — but that script cannot fix the build-lock gate. The script docstring likewise only documents the two app/core locks.

4. requirements-build.lock pins trove-classifiers==2026.6.1.19, a frequently-released transitive dep of hatchling, confirming the staleness cadence concern is real and reachable.

This is a genuine OPS-dimension consistency defect: the single documented, script-based remediation referenced by both dependabot.yml and the script's own docstring is incomplete relative to the CI gate it is meant to satisfy. A maintainer following the documented workflow regenerates two locks and remains red, with no committed script path to regenerate the third.

Possible mitigations considered and rejected: (a) Not a false positive — the gap is exact and reproducible. (b) Not already-mitigated — no other script regenerates the build lock. (c) Not out-of-scope-by-design — this is CI/build tooling, not the dataset-agnostic engine, optional backends, scrubbed transcripts, or on-box data sovereignty. (d) The only mitigating nuance is that the CI error message itself prints the exact one-off command, so manual recovery is possible — but the finding explicitly accounts for this ("they must hand-reconstruct the exact CI command") and its claim is about the documented *script* remediation being broken, which is true. This nuance fits the assigned medium/OPS severity (a tooling-consistency dead-end, not a hard impossibility), so it does not refute the finding.

The finding STANDS.

survivors.title: Coverage is measured but never enforced — the integration coverage report is a vacuous gate
survivors.claim: The integration job runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. There is no `--cov-fail-under` flag on the command, no `[tool.coverage.report] fail_under` in pyproject.toml, and no .coveragerc (confirmed: grep for `cov-fail-under|fail_under|fail-under` across ci.yml and pyproject.toml finds nothing; no .coveragerc exists). pytest --cov without a threshold exits 0 regardless of how low coverage is.
survivors.why_real: For a project that has run seven hardening passes and explicitly added pytest-cov to gate coverage (#103), the coverage number is printed to logs but enforces nothing: a refactor that deletes tests or that adds a large untested module would still pass CI green. The gate exists in appearance only — exactly the 'job passes vacuously' failure mode for this dimension. The coverage signal cannot catch a regression because nothing acts on it.
survivors.suggested_fix: Add an enforced floor, e.g. `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing --cov-fail-under=<current%>` (or a `[tool.coverage.report] fail_under = N` block in pyproject.toml), set to roughly the current coverage so future drops fail CI. Optionally add `--cov-report=xml` and upload it as an artifact for trend visibility.

refute.reasoning:
The finding is factually accurate and holds up against scrutiny. Verified directly in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay:

1. .github/workflows/ci.yml:106 runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` with NO `--cov-fail-under` flag.
2. pyproject.toml contains only `pytest-cov>=5` (line 41) with a comment "CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` (#103)" — there is no `[tool.coverage.report] fail_under` and no `[tool.coverage]` section at all.
3. No `.coveragerc`, no `setup.cfg`, no `tox.ini` exist (confirmed via ls — all absent).

The technical claim is correct: `pytest --cov` without a threshold prints coverage but exits 0 regardless of how low it is. Nothing in the repo acts on the coverage number, so it cannot fail CI. A refactor that deletes tests or adds a large untested module would still pass green. The pyproject.toml comment explicitly frames pytest-cov as added "to gate coverage (#103)", yet the configuration does not gate — it only reports. This is precisely the "vacuous gate / passes vacuously" failure mode the finding describes.

I considered refutation paths: (a) out-of-scope by design — not applicable; this is a generic CI hygiene concern, not tied to dataset-agnostic engine, optional backends, scrubbed transcripts, or on-box sovereignty. (b) already-mitigated — no, there is no threshold anywhere. (c) false positive — no, the grep results and file absence confirm the claim exactly. (d) value still provided by measurement — coverage visibility has some value, but the finding does not claim the report is useless; it claims the GATE is vacuous, which is true. The stated rationale (the coverage signal cannot catch a regression because nothing acts on it) is correct. This is a real, if modest, OPS defect at the asserted medium severity. Cannot refute.

survivors.title: SIGTERM trace-flush handler makes the study process unkillable by SIGTERM (swallows default terminate)
survivors.claim: After bootstrap_tracing() runs on the main thread, it installs a SIGTERM handler that flushes/shuts down the tracer and then only re-invokes the prior handler `if callable(prior)`. Python's default SIGTERM disposition is signal.SIG_DFL == 0, which is NOT callable, so the chain falls through and the process does NOT terminate on SIGTERM. A `kill <pid>` / `docker stop` / k8s pod SIGTERM flushes traces but leaves the process running, requiring SIGKILL.
survivors.why_real: Reproduced directly: with prior=signal.getsignal(SIGTERM)=0 (SIG_DFL, callable()==False), sending os.kill(pid, SIGTERM) flushed but the process printed 'STILL ALIVE AFTER SIGTERM' and exited 0 only because the script ended. In a long-running study this converts graceful SIGTERM into a no-op; orchestrators that send SIGTERM-then-wait hang until their SIGKILL grace timeout. The fix-comment claims the handler 'also flushes+shuts down' but never preserves the default-terminate semantics, silently breaking normal process termination for every traced run.
survivors.suggested_fix: After flush/shutdown, if prior is callable invoke it; else replicate the prior disposition: for SIG_DFL re-raise the default action (reset handler to SIG_DFL and os.kill(os.getpid(), signum) or raise SystemExit), for SIG_IGN do nothing. Do not silently return when prior is SIG_DFL.

refute.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code at src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:136-158. The _install_flush_on_exit._handler runs _safe(flush) then _safe(shutdown), then `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)`. The default SIGTERM disposition is signal.SIG_DFL, which I confirmed at runtime is NOT callable (callable(signal.SIG_DFL) == False, and signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM) == SIG_DFL on a fresh process). Therefore the `callable(prior)` branch is skipped and the process never performs the default terminate action. I reproduced this end-to-end: a script replicating the exact handler logic, after os.kill(pid, SIGTERM), printed FLUSHED, SHUTDOWN, then STILL ALIVE AFTER SIGTERM and continued running — proving SIGTERM is swallowed into a no-op.

This is wired into the real production run path: bootstrap_tracing() is called at pipeline.py:381 (and orchestration/graph.py:111/139), invokes _install_flush_on_exit(provider) at tracing.py:97, which installs the handler when threading.current_thread() is the main thread — the study startup thread. The handler is only installed when tracing is enabled AND the observability extra/Phoenix register() succeeds (the intended traced production configuration). When present, a `kill <pid>`/`docker stop`/k8s pod SIGTERM flushes traces but leaves the long-running study process alive, forcing orchestrators to wait out their grace period and SIGKILL.

None of the REFUTED exemptions apply: this is not an optional-backend concern in spirit (it is core process-lifecycle behavior that fires precisely in the supported traced config), not dataset-specific, not test/transcript scope, and not a false positive. The docstring even claims the handler "flushes+shuts down" on SIGTERM but it silently fails to preserve default-terminate semantics. To fix, the default branch should re-raise default termination (e.g., reset to SIG_DFL and re-raise, or sys.exit/os._exit) when prior is SIG_DFL, and ignore when prior is SIG_IGN. Finding stands.

survivors.title: Provenance artifact silently dropped and temp file leaked when a payload holds a hashable-but-non-JSON value
survivors.claim: ProvenanceTrail.record() accepts any *hashable* leaf (freeze() only rejects unhashable values), so bytes/datetime/Enum/custom-hashable values in a payload (e.g. a gate evidence dict, line 174 as_recorder) are stored intact and survive unfreeze() unchanged. In _log_trail, json.dump(entries_to_records(...)) then raises TypeError before `path = fh.name` is reached, so (a) the NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False) is orphaned on disk with provenance contents, and (b) track() catches the TypeError and records only a generic 'tracking_error' entry — the #G-009 provenance artifact never attaches to the MLflow run.
survivors.why_real: Reproduced: trail.record('gate','g', evidence={'token': b'..rawbytes', 'when': datetime(...)}) is accepted by freeze, then json.dumps(entries_to_records(...)) raises 'Object of type bytes is not JSON serializable'. This contradicts both the #H-013 fix intent ('a tracked run does not leak a temp file') — the cleanup try/finally at line 748 is unreachable because `path` is never assigned — and the #G-009 guarantee that an auditor opening the run can retrieve the provenance that produced its metrics. Failure is silent (no _log.warning, unlike the start_run path).
survivors.suggested_fix: Wrap the temp-file lifecycle in try/finally keyed off the file object (or use delete=True after a fsync/copy), and have _log_trail use a JSON-safe serializer (default=str / reject non-JSON values at record() time). Emit a _log.warning when the provenance artifact cannot be serialized, matching the start_run failure path.

refute.reasoning:
Confirmed by direct reproduction against the actual code. The finding holds on all three mechanical claims:

1. freeze() accepts hashable-but-non-JSON leaves. In src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:82-126, freeze() converts bytearray->bytes and array-likes, but a raw bytes or datetime value is hashable, so it passes the hash(value) check and is returned UNCHANGED as a leaf. At record() time, _digest (provenance.py:69-91) hashes the payload via canonical_json, which DOES handle bytes (__bytes__ hex) and datetime (__temporal__) — so record() does NOT reject them. Reproduced: trail.record('gate','g', evidence={'token': b'rawbytes', 'when': datetime(...)}) is accepted.

2. The value survives unfreeze() unchanged and breaks json.dump. entries_to_records (provenance.py:211-240) thaws via unfreeze(), which returns leaves unchanged (_frozen.py:164). The bytes value reaches json.dump as raw bytes. Reproduced: json.dumps(entries_to_records(...)) raises 'Object of type bytes is not JSON serializable'.

3. Both consequences confirmed:
   (a) TEMP FILE LEAK — in _log_trail (pipeline.py:740-754), json.dump(...) at line 743 runs inside the `with NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False)` block BEFORE `path = fh.name` (line 744). When json.dump raises, path is never assigned; the with-block exits leaving the file on disk (delete=False); the try/finally cleanup at 748-754 is unreachable because it follows the with-block. Reproduced the leak directly: a *_provenance.json file remained on disk containing the provenance entries, with path == None. This directly contradicts the #H-013 fix intent ('a tracked run does not leak a temp file').
   (b) SILENT DROP — track() (pipeline.py:317-324) catches the TypeError and records only a generic trail.record('tracking_error', ...) entry, with NO _log.warning — unlike the start_run failure path (pipeline.py:364-369) which DOES emit _log.warning. So the #G-009 provenance artifact never attaches to the MLflow run and the operator gets no log signal.

Scoping defenses do not save this finding:
- Reachability is real: as_recorder (provenance.py:162-188) builds gate 'evidence' from a caller/adapter-supplied decision.evidence dict — datetime timestamps or bytes tokens/signatures are entirely plausible real evidence, and the freeze() contract explicitly permits any hashable leaf, so this is documented-as-allowed input the serialization path cannot handle.
- The tracker path is guarded by `tracker is not None`, but that is the NORMAL observability configuration, not an optional out-of-scope backend; when a tracker is wired (the intended #G-009 audit case) a single such evidence value both leaks a provenance-bearing temp file and silently loses the provenance artifact.
- The #H-013 regression test (tests/test_pipeline.py:436-458) only exercises JSON-native evidence on the happy path, so this failure mode is genuinely uncovered.

This is a real production defect that simultaneously breaks the #H-013 (no temp-file leak) and #G-009 (provenance artifact attaches for audit) guarantees, silently. The fix is straightforward: serialize to a string first / use json.dumps(..., default=...) or canonical_json, assign path before any work that can raise, and warn on failure.

refuted_ids.why:
The finding's code-logic claim is accurate (_display_name_patterns() returns [] when all of {GIT_AUTHOR_NAME, GIT_COMMITTER_NAME, git config user.name, git log -1 --format=%an} are empty, and _scrub then no-ops on names — I reproduced exactly "DISPLAY pats: []" and an unredacted "[REDACTED]" in a zero-commit `git init` repo). I also confirmed the operator's machine has no local and no global git user.name. However, the claimed PRODUCTION leak scenario cannot actually occur, for two converging reasons:

1. The README's instruction (`git config core.hooksPath .githooks`) is for a FRESH CLONE — and a clone carries the full commit history, so `git log -1 --format=%an` succeeds and returns "[REDACTED]". I tested this literally: in a fresh `git clone` of the repo, with no env vars and no local/global user.name, _display_name_patterns() returns the three correct patterns and _scrub('[REDACTED] approves') -> '[REDACTED] approves'. The finding conflates "fresh clone" (has history -> works) with "git init, zero commits" (no history). The README never instructs the latter, and the existing corpus is committed (so any clone of it has history). The premise "in a fresh clone with no commits yet (git log -1 fails)" is self-contradictory: a fresh clone of this repo always has commits.

2. The only context where git log -1 genuinely fails is a brand-new `git init` with zero commits. But in that context the leak still cannot land, because to MAKE a commit (which is what triggers the pre-commit hook to write+stage a transcript) git requires a resolvable author identity. I verified that with empty user.name and no env override, git aborts with "Author identity unknown" and writes nothing. If the author is resolvable, it comes from a source the scrubber also reads: `git config user.name` (read directly), `GIT_AUTHOR_NAME` env (read at lines 93-96), or — I tested the `git commit --author="[REDACTED]"` override path — git itself exports GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=[[REDACTED]] into the hook environment, which the scrubber picks up. Thus the two conditions the exploit requires (a commit successfully lands AND the scrubber pattern set is empty) cannot co-occur: git's author-resolution sources are a superset of the scrubber's name-derivation sources.

The scrubber is intentionally multi-source and fail-open-but-not-silently-broken in any real commit path. The asserted silent fail-open ("exits 0, writes files, redacts nothing for the name dimension") does not arise in any context that actually produces a committed transcript. This is a false positive / non-reachable in production. Files examined: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py (lines 76-133, 192, 196-243), /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.githooks/pre-commit, /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/transcripts/README.md.

The finding's narrow code claim is literally true: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:73 returns re.compile(re.escape(name)) with NO re.IGNORECASE, whereas the display-name (lines 121,132) and handle (185,187) rules use re.IGNORECASE. The compiled username pattern matches '[REDACTED]' but not 'Thomas[REDACTED]'/'THOMAS[REDACTED]'. So an asymmetry exists.

However, the finding is REFUTED as a real production defect because its claimed leak vectors do not hold up against the actual code and data:

1. Asserted vectors are already covered by OTHER rules:
   - git author strings: the operator's git name is "[REDACTED]" (spaced), redacted by the IGNORECASE display-name rule which also redacts the bare tokens "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" case-insensitively.
   - email local-parts: [REDACTED]@... is fully removed by the email regex (line 50) regardless of case.
   - /Users paths and project-dir slugs: redacted by the STRUCTURAL path/slug rules (lines 51,55), which are case-derived only for the literal "Users"/"home" keyword, not the username — verified '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc' -> '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc'.

2. The username token is '[REDACTED]' by construction: Path.home().name on this node is the lowercase home-dir name (verified). The organic corpus contains it lowercase 234,930 times and in mixed case ZERO times. The only mixed-case occurrences (17 Thomas[REDACTED] + 10 THOMAS[REDACTED]) live in 3 files all timestamped today (2026-06-17) inside this very audit session's transcript — they are the auditor's own self-referential discussion of the bug, not organic operator PII. Before this audit, no mixed-case username occurrence existed.

3. Even in the constructed mixed-case case, _scrub('Thomas[REDACTED]') -> '[REDACTED][REDACTED]' (the IGNORECASE "[REDACTED]" display-token catches the tail; only a generic first-name fragment "[REDACTED]" remains, itself a display-token-boundary artifact, not the operator-unique username token). The finding's scenario of "redacted lowercase username beside a cleartext CamelCase variant that re-identifies the operator" does not occur on real data.

Net: a real but theoretical/cosmetic inconsistency with no demonstrable PII leak on the actual corpus; adding IGNORECASE would be harmless hardening but the medium-severity PRIV leak premise does not hold.

The finding accurately describes the regex behavior (I reproduced both example over-redactions), but it does not hold up as a real production defect, for three independent reasons:

1. Out-of-scope by design (intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts / dev tooling). scripts/capture_transcripts.py is a pre-commit-hook provenance scrubber, NOT part of the shipped product. Its own docstring states "The wheel never ships them (only src/assay_engine is packaged)." This is exactly the "intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts" exclusion. The separator-flexible branch is a deliberate over-redaction-biased PII defense (#J-008 round 3): the explicit design choice is to redact MORE rather than risk leaking the operator's brand. Over-redaction is the intended failure mode, not a defect.

2. The finding's realistic-looking trigger is actually the intended behavior. "[REDACTED] reviewed the policy." fully redacts — but the code comment at line 178-184 explicitly designs for this: the SPACED source brand ("[REDACTED]") is precisely what must be redacted so a reader cannot reconstruct the de-spaced handle "[REDACTED]" by concatenation. So the most plausible real-world hit is the defense working as designed, not corruption of unrelated content.

3. The remaining "harm" cases are contrived and astronomically improbable. The finding's true false-positive examples (`draft. [REDACTED].`, `section [REDACTED] appendix`) require legitimate prose to spell the operator's exact 14-letter handle in order with ONLY [\s._-] between every character. I verified the separator class is narrow: any digit, comma, or other letter breaks the run (`the,hi,ps...`, `the9hipster9ciso`, `xthex...` all pass through unredacted). An acrostic/spaced run matching the full handle does not occur in genuine transcript content. The finding itself concedes it is not catastrophic backtracking (linear, ~3ms/200k chars).

The behavior is real but it is an intentional, conservative over-redaction in a non-shipped provenance tool whose realistic trigger is the very brand-reconstruction it was built to defend against. Default-to-REFUTED applies: this is not a real production correctness defect.

The finding's mechanical claim is technically accurate — _username_pattern() (scripts/capture_transcripts.py:59-73) uses Path.home().name with only a `len < 4` guard, returns re.compile(re.escape(name)) with no \b boundary, so a foreign HOME like /tmp/ci-test yields re.compile('ci\\-test') as a substring match. But this is not a real production defect:

1) Out-of-scope by design — the capture tool is an operator/dev script (no CI wiring found; grep shows only test references in tests/test_scripts.py, never a workflow). It runs ON the operator's box (per memory, this Mac mini is the production node, operator [REDACTED]) reading from the operator's Claude session dir under HOME. In the actual capture context, Path.home().name correctly equals the operator username by design. The "foreign/CI HOME" scenario is hypothetical and not part of the real flow.

2) The "ZERO protection for [REDACTED] in CI" claim is moot: in a clean /tmp/ci-test HOME there is no [REDACTED] content/session to scrub — the source transcripts live under the operator's real HOME, so the username derivation and the content being scrubbed are coupled to the SAME HOME. 3) Defense-in-depth: the username rule is explicitly a supplementary bare-token catch (#G-007). PII is also covered by the /Users|/home path rule (line 51), the -Users-[REDACTED]- slug rule (line 55), display-name rules (ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES / git user.name, lines 76-92, 192/210-211), and repo-handle rules (193/214-215). 4) The over-redaction the finding raises is conceded by the finding itself as "harmless-to-wrong" and has no security/correctness consequence — redacting the literal string of the runtime home name causes no leak.

Severity is already low/PRIV. The behavior is an intentional HOME-derived design choice for an on-box operator tool, not a production defect that leaks or corrupts data in its real execution context.

The finding's described mechanism is technically accurate (I reproduced it: a NUL-free invalid-UTF-8 blob like b"\xff\xd8\xff\xe0binarydata\xc2\xa9\xff" is rewritten to U+FFFD bytes, 17 -> 27 bytes), but it is NOT a real production defect. It is REFUTED as out-of-scope-by-design / not a real defect for these reasons:

1. The input population is text-only by construction. capture_transcripts.py captures the active Claude session and its <id>/ subtree (scripts/capture_transcripts.py:7-8, 271-279). These are Claude agent transcripts: JSON/JSONL/txt/js/md. I enumerated the actual production source tree (~/.claude/projects/-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): 3263 files, of which 1590 .jsonl, 1579 .json, 52 .txt, 34 .js, 6 .md — ZERO image/gzip/binary artifacts. The finding's hypothesized inputs (JPEG fragments, gzip members, latin-1 subprocess dumps committed as standalone files) do not occur in this subtree; subprocess stdout lives embedded as escaped strings INSIDE the JSONL, not as standalone binary files.

2. The exact "dangerous class" the finding relies on is empirically empty. I classified all 3263 real files in Python: files containing NUL = 0; NUL-free-but-invalid-UTF-8 (the lossy-rewrite class) = 0. So in production the else-branch only ever receives valid UTF-8 text, which round-trips losslessly; the lossy path is never exercised.

3. It is an intentional, documented, tested trade-off — not a silent regression. The code comment (lines 233-239) explicitly explains J-001 chose to FAIL CLOSED on redaction: the prior decode-validity gate was fail-OPEN, where a single stray byte routed the WHOLE transcript to the verbatim branch and re-leaked operator PII/credential-shaped secrets (the CV-O-1 fail-open bug). The maintainers deliberately weighed "verbatim-preserve a hypothetical NUL-free binary" against "guarantee redaction always runs on text" and chose redaction integrity. tests/test_scripts.py:116-144 codify exactly this: binary-with-NUL copied verbatim (line 123), and invalid-UTF-8 text still scrubbed (lines 127-144). This is by design, with NUL chosen as the binary signal precisely because real binaries reliably contain NUL while text transcripts do not.

4. The stated provenance guarantee is not defeated for any real artifact: every committed file's sha256 is over the bytes actually written, the manifest is internally consistent, and for the only file types that exist (valid UTF-8 text after redaction), committed bytes equal source bytes modulo the intentional secret/PII redaction that is the whole point of _scrub. The "provenance" guarantee was never "byte-identical to raw" — the docstring (lines 9-13) and patterns (lines 38-56) make clear redaction is applied on the way in by design.

In short: the regression is real only against inputs that cannot occur in the in-scope text-transcript subtree, and the chosen behavior is the deliberate, tested resolution of a worse fail-open secret-leak. Not a production defect.

The finding's literal facts check out, but its characterization as a defect does not. Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:225-254 and tests/test_scripts.py:116-124:

- Fixture `b"\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary"` does contain `\x00`, so `_copy_scrubbed` takes the verbatim branch (line 240-241). Confirmed empirically.
- A NUL-free undecodable input (e.g. `b"\xff\xfebinary"`) is NOT preserved: it decodes with errors="replace" and re-encodes to `b"\xef\xbf\xbd\xef\xbf\xbdbinary"`. Confirmed empirically.

However the finding is REFUTED because it mischaracterizes both the guarded invariant and "the K-P7-1 corruption regression":

1. K-P7-1/J-001 (docs/hardening/PASS-7.md:22) was a fail-OPEN (re-leak) regression, NOT a corruption regression. The fix's invariant, stated in the code comment (lines 233-239), is: "redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL present) is copied verbatim." The code's operational definition of "genuinely binary" IS "NUL present." The fixture matches that definition exactly, so the test correctly exercises the verbatim branch for precisely the class of files the code is designed to copy verbatim. It would correctly fail if someone broke that branch (e.g. started scrubbing NUL files).

2. "Binary preservation works" for NUL-free undecodable files is NOT a guarantee the code makes — by explicit design a NUL-free file is treated as text and scrubbed. Preserving such a file byte-exact would be the fail-OPEN behavior J-001 deliberately eliminated. So the "corruption" the finding posits is the intended, fail-closed path, not a regression of any committed invariant.

3. The fail-closed direction the finding worries about (stray-byte/undecodable text still redacted) IS covered, by test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes (line 126) and test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 (line 138).

The finding's strongest point ("removing \x00 flips the test to FAILING") actually proves the code intentionally does not copy NUL-free files verbatim — i.e. it confirms correct design, not a blind test. At most this is imprecise wording in a test docstring ("genuinely binary (undecodable)" vs the code's NUL-based definition), which is a cosmetic nit, not a production defect or a meaningful coverage gap. Test runs green; the regression it should guard (verbatim for NUL blobs / scrub for everything else) is in fact guarded across the suite.

The finding's load-bearing premise is false. It claims the build-lock inputs are uniquely "unconstrained by any pyproject range," in contrast to the surrounding requirements.lock/requirements-core.lock regen steps which it implies ARE constrained — and concludes the build-lock branch "reintroduces a known spurious-CI-failure mode" the others avoid. Reading the actual files refutes this:

1. pyproject.toml (/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml) declares ALL dependencies with version RANGES, not exact pins: `dependencies = []` for the core, and extras like `pytest>=8`, `mypy>=1.11`, `langgraph>=1.2,<2`, etc. The build-system requires is just `["hatchling"]` — same unconstrained shape as the runtime deps.

2. The very transitive deps the finding cites as the build-lock's unique liability — `packaging==26.2`, `pathspec==1.1.1`, `pluggy==1.6.0`, and `trove-classifiers`-class time-versioned packages — also appear pinned as PURELY TRANSITIVE entries in BOTH main locks: requirements.lock:2460 `packaging==26.2 # via gunicorn / langchain-core / langsmith`, and requirements-core.lock:296 `packaging==26.2 # via pytest`, plus pathspec `# via mypy` and pluggy `# via pytest`. None of these are pinned by a pyproject `==`, so `uv pip compile pyproject.toml` resolves latest-compatible exactly as `uv pip compile build-reqs.in` does.

3. Therefore there is NO asymmetry: when packaging 26.3 (or any transitive bump) releases, all three regen+diff steps fail identically. The build-lock step did not introduce a new failure class; it shares the exact same property as the pre-existing, accepted main-lock steps — and shares the identical mitigation the workflow relies on (pinning `uv==0.11.21` and `pip-audit==2.10.1`, ci.yml:128-131), which the comment explicitly frames as the cure for "transient resolver/index differences ... that fail this gate on every PR." That pin is process-wide, not main-lock-specific.

The finding rests on a false distinction between constrained main locks and unconstrained build locks; the code shows they are equivalent by design. Lockfile staleness gates that pin both the resolver (uv) and the index-resolved set are a deliberate reproducibility tradeoff applied uniformly, not a defect reintroduced for the build lock. Not a real production defect — out-of-scope by design / false premise.

The finding's factual surface is accurate: the verdict trail entries (pipeline.py:577-583 and 649-655) record only hypothesis_id, label, and the decision_rule string; the per-verdict evidence (p_support, p_contradict, statistic, stability, etc., built at confirm.py:409-418) is NOT passed into trail.record(), so it is not part of any entry_hash (provenance.py _digest hashes seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/secret). I confirmed this.

But the finding's stated threat model — "an actor who can mutate the serialized StudyResult ... can alter the p-values/statistic ... and verify_records on the trail still passes" — does not hold against the actual engine, and is the load-bearing part of the "Why real":

1. The engine never serializes StudyResult's verdict evidence to any durable artifact. StudyResult.persist_trail() (pipeline.py:226-244) writes ONLY self.provenance (the hash-chained entries) to disk via entries_to_records; it does not persist discovery_verdicts, scorecard, or any evidence numbers. A grep of pipeline.py shows zero references to .evidence/p_support/per-verdict .statistic in any record() call or any serialization path. There is therefore no persisted file containing the evidence for a reviewer to "re-derive from" and for an attacker to tamper while leaving verify_records passing. verify_records operates on the trail, not on StudyResult; the attack targets an artifact the engine does not produce.

2. The decision-bearing OUTPUTS that matter for integrity ARE hash-bound: every per-verdict label is recorded into the chain, and the aggregate scorecard (n_supported/n_contradicted/n_indeterminate/alignment_rate, pipeline.py:672-678) is a chained entry. Flipping a label would mismatch the recorded aggregate score entry, and forging both is what the HMAC-keyed tier prevents.

3. The evidence mappings live only as frozen fields on an in-memory frozen StudyResult dataclass. GOVERNANCE §3 explicitly scopes this: "An in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process." The evidence is in-process state, identically to the Verdict object itself, and is exactly the scope §3 declares out of the tamper-evidence guarantee.

The only residual is that ADR-0004 / verdict.py's docstring says evidence is carried "so a reviewer can re-derive it" — but that is a property of the in-memory/returned Verdict object (which does carry full evidence in Verdict.evidence), not a claim that the evidence is committed to the hash chain. The engine delivers the evidence to the reviewer on the live StudyResult; it does not promise to make the evidence tamper-evident in a durable artifact, and it does not emit such a durable artifact at all. The claimed integrity violation (durable trail silently forgeable while passing verify_records) is constructed against a serialized-evidence artifact that does not exist. This is a false-positive threat model plus an in-memory-scope concern that GOVERNANCE §3 already declares out of scope. Not a real production defect.

The finding's literal facts are correct but its harm theory does not hold, so it is not a real production defect on the adjudicate() path.

Verified facts: adjudicate() (src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:103-181) materializes claims_source.claims() at line 165 and never calls claim_fingerprint() (grep finds zero fingerprint references in adjudication.py). The recompute-and-enforce check (FirewallViolation on mismatch) exists only in pipeline.py:603-619. adjudicate is a public, documented entry point (exported in methodology/__init__.py:17,57; first-class in ADR-0008).

Why REFUTED:
1. adjudicate()'s OWN docstring (lines 113-143) enumerates exactly three enforced guarantees — signature-level Firewall A, claim<->hypothesis<->verdict identity + pre-registration, and EXTERNAL_CLAIM typing — and never mentions the fingerprint. ADR-0008 (the authoritative design doc) likewise lists the adjudicate() guarantees (items 1-4) and does not include the fingerprint. So the entry point does not over-promise the check.
2. The unscoped "the engine recomputes... raises FirewallViolation" wording lives in claims.py:75-84 (the ExternalClaimsSource contract), which is at most a documentation imprecision attributing to "the engine" generically what only the pipeline does. It is not a code defect.
3. Critically, the breach F-001 actually closed was the RECORDING of an unverified/lying fingerprint into the run's provenance artifact under a non-interactive auto_approve gate (PASS-3.md:37-39). adjudicate() has no gate, records no fingerprint, and consumes no fingerprint: it returns (baseline, scorecard) where the scorecard is computed over `materialized = claims_source.claims()` — i.e., exactly the claims that are scored. There is no separate attested set and no persisted commitment that a false fingerprint could launder. The finding's claimed harm ("attest to one set and adjudicate another") is hollow because the fingerprint is never consulted, recorded, or relied upon on this path — there is nothing to disagree with.

Thus the provenance-integrity breach F-001 was created to close (a recorded false fingerprint laundered through auto_approve) structurally cannot occur on the adjudicate() path. The only residual is a too-broad sentence in the claims.py contract docstring, which is a doc-tightening nit, not a production provenance defect. Out-of-scope/not-a-real-defect -> REFUTED.

The finding's mechanics are factually correct — confirm_unit_level (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:155-216) decides supported/contradicted SOLELY on the caller's direction_supports_claim flag (verdict_from_pvalue, lines 148-151) and only shape-validates the locked predicted_direction (lines 198-204), while preregistration.py:117 binds predicted_direction into the digest. The demonstrated scenario (locked 'greater', statistic=-99.0, flag=True yields 'supported') is real. However, this is out-of-scope-by-design / documented honest-scope, not a production defect:

1. It is prominently and explicitly documented in the function docstring (confirm.py:172-187): the unit-level verdict is "governed SOLELY by the caller-supplied direction_supports_claim flag (the engine sees only an opaque held-out statistic/p_value and cannot itself infer the tail)"; predicted_direction "is therefore advisory at unit level... but does NOT decide the verdict"; and "A study that wants the locked tail to bind the unit-level decision must derive direction_supports_claim from it in its own confirmer."

2. The whole-corpus/unit-level asymmetry is itself documented as intentional (#H-015, confirm.py:180-183): because the locked direction never drives the unit-level verdict, a directionless lock is harmless, "the asymmetry is intentional, not a missing guard."

3. The behavior is locked by a discriminating regression test (tests/test_confirm.py:338, test_unit_level_verdict_is_governed_by_caller_flag_not_locked_direction, #G-017) that asserts CONTRADICTED when the flag is False despite a locked 'greater' — codifying the design.

The engine is deliberately dataset/test-agnostic and receives only an opaque statistic, so it structurally cannot reconcile the statistic against the locked tail without becoming test-aware. The finding identifies no incorrect behavior, regression, or undocumented gap — it restates an already-documented, already-tested, intentionally-scoped limitation. The finding itself concedes severity is low and that it is "documented as intentional honest-scope (#H-015)... a known limitation rather than a hidden bug." Per the rubric (out-of-scope by design + already-mitigated/documented), this is REFUTED: there is no production defect to fix.

The code behaves exactly as the finding describes — I reproduced it: a file containing `[REDACTED]`, `[REDACTED]`, and a raw `\x00` byte was copied verbatim by `_copy_scrubbed` (scripts/capture_transcripts.py:240-243), leaking both token and email. So the mechanism is real in isolation. But the finding is REFUTED as a real production defect because its load-bearing premise does not hold for this scrubber's actual input domain.

The scrubber only ever processes Claude Code session transcripts: the active top-level `*.jsonl` plus the session's `<id>/` subtree of `.jsonl`/`.json` subagent+workflow transcripts (main() lines 257-279; docstring lines 1-9, 29). These files are written by Claude Code as well-formed JSON/JSONL. The finding's "why real" rationale is that subprocess stdout embedded in JSONL string values can carry a raw NUL (binary dumps, `cat` of binaries, terminal control bytes). That is false for JSON: a literal NUL in a string is invalid JSON and is serialized as the 6-character ASCII escape `�`, never as a raw `0x00` byte. I verified this empirically — `json.dumps({"content":"...\x00..."})` produces `\\u0000` and the encoded line contains zero raw NUL bytes. The `b"\x00" in raw` test therefore cannot fire on any genuine transcript line; it only matches a truly binary, non-JSON blob (the documented intent, covered by test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim with `\xff\xfe\x00\x01`).

I also scanned the entire committed transcript corpus (transcripts/**/*.jsonl, *.json) and the live source dir (~/.claude/projects) for raw NUL bytes via `grep -P "\x00"` — zero matches anywhere. The finding itself concedes "No committed transcript currently contains a NUL (latent, not yet materialized)." The supposed materialization path (binary subprocess stdout) is precisely the case JSON escaping neutralizes, so it cannot materialize through normal transcript capture. Invalid-UTF-8 bytes (the realistic stray-byte case) are already handled fail-closed via `decode(errors="replace")` and are covered by tests test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes and test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8. The NUL branch is an intentional, narrowly-scoped don't-corrupt-true-binary safeguard, not a fail-open redaction hole reachable by the documented input. Latent, non-materializable, out-of-scope for this dataset-agnostic JSON-transcript pipeline — not a real production defect.
